question: [File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: throttle(ContractCallRequest)] Can an unprivileged external user submit a ContractCallRequest with gas set exactly to the GAS_SCALE_FACTOR boundary (10,000) so that scaleGas() returns 0, causing gasLimitBucket.tryConsume(0) to always succeed and completely bypassing the gas-per-second throttle, allowing unlimited high-cost EVM executions?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: throttle(ContractCallRequest)] Can an unprivileged external user set gas=21000 (the minimum allowed by @Min(21_000)) in ContractCallRequest so that scaleGas(21000) returns 2 (floor(21000/10000)), consuming only 2 tokens from gasLimitBucket per request instead of proportional cost, enabling the attacker to submit far more high-gas-equivalent transactions than the throttle intends to allow?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: restore(long)] Can an unprivileged external user deliberately trigger an InvalidParametersException after throttle() succeeds (e.g., by submitting gas above evmProperties.getMaxGasLimit()) so that ContractController.call() calls throttleManager.restore(request.getGas()) with the full gas value, artificially inflating the gasLimitBucket beyond its intended capacity and allowing subsequent requests to bypass gas throttling?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: throttle(ContractCallRequest)] Can an unprivileged external user set estimate=true in ContractCallRequest so that ContractCallService.doProcessCall() skips the restoreGasToBucket() call entirely, meaning consumed gas tokens are never returned to gasLimitBucket after execution, causing legitimate users to be throttled while the attacker's estimate calls drain the gas budget without real cost?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: throttle(ContractCallRequest)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that rateLimitBucket.tryConsume(1) is checked before gasLimitBucket.tryConsume(), so when gasLimitBucket fails the rateLimitBucket token is already consumed but never restored, allowing an attacker to deliberately exhaust the gas bucket to force all subsequent legitimate requests to fail the rate limit check as well, causing a cascading denial of service?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: restore(long)] Can an unprivileged external user craft a sequence of requests where each triggers an InvalidParametersException (gas > maxGasLimit) causing restore(request.getGas()) to be called repeatedly, and since Bucket.addTokens() in bucket4j adds tokens up to the bucket capacity without overflow protection at the application level, the attacker can keep the gasLimitBucket perpetually full, preventing the throttle from ever being effective?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: throttle(ContractCallRequest)] Can an unprivileged external user send concurrent requests exploiting the fact that rateLimitBucket and opcodeRateLimitBucket are created without SynchronizationStrategy.SYNCHRONIZED (unlike gasLimitBucket), causing race conditions in tryConsume() that allow more requests to pass the rate limit check than the configured requestsPerSecond allows?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: throttle(ContractCallRequest)] Can an unprivileged external user craft a ContractCallRequest with a data field containing a specific byte sequence that matches a configured RequestFilter with FilterType.CONTAINS and ActionType.LOG, causing the full request (including from, to, data, gas, value fields) to be logged at INFO level, leaking sensitive transaction details to log aggregation systems accessible to other parties?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: throttle(ContractCallRequest)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the case-insensitive FilterType.EQUALS and FilterType.CONTAINS matching in RequestFilter.test() to craft a from or to address that matches a REJECT filter using mixed-case hex characters (e.g., '0xAbCd...' vs '0xabcd...'), bypassing the filter and having the transaction processed when it should have been rejected?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: throttle(ContractCallRequest)] Can an unprivileged external user set gas to Long.MAX_VALUE (if validation allows values near the max) so that scaleGas() computes Math.floorDiv(Long.MAX_VALUE, 10000) producing a very large token count, causing gasLimitBucket.tryConsume() to always fail with GAS_PER_SECOND_LIMIT_EXCEEDED, effectively denying service to all other users by exhausting the shared gas bucket in a single request?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: throttle(ContractCallRequest)] Can an unprivileged external user submit a ContractCallRequest with estimate=true and maximum gas to drain the gasLimitBucket, then immediately submit real (non-estimate) calls with minimum gas (21000) that consume only 2 tokens each, exploiting the asymmetry between gas consumed during throttle check and actual EVM execution cost to perform expensive operations while bypassing proportional throttling?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: throttle(ContractCallRequest)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the ordering in throttle() where request filters are evaluated after rate and gas limit checks, so that a REJECT filter intended to block certain transaction patterns is never reached if the attacker first exhausts the rateLimitBucket, causing the filter logic to be silently skipped while the attacker's request is rejected for a different reason, masking the filter bypass?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: throttle(ContractCallRequest)] Can an unprivileged external user manipulate the block field in ContractCallRequest (e.g., setting it to a specific historical block number) to avoid matching a RequestFilter configured with FilterField.BLOCK and FilterType.EQUALS targeting 'latest', thereby bypassing a REJECT or THROTTLE action intended to restrict calls to the latest block?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: throttle(ContractCallRequest)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the AtomicLong counter in RequestProperties.test() which increments unconditionally via getAndIncrement(), causing the counter to eventually overflow to Long.MIN_VALUE after 2^63 requests, making counter.getAndIncrement() >= limit evaluate to false (since MIN_VALUE < MAX_VALUE), effectively resetting the limit enforcement and allowing previously-blocked request patterns to pass through again?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: throttle(ContractCallRequest)] Can an unprivileged external user craft a ContractCallRequest where the data field is null (omitted from JSON body), causing RequestFilter with FilterField.DATA to call field.getExtractor().apply(request) returning null, and then String.valueOf(null) producing the string 'null', which could accidentally match a CONTAINS filter expression containing 'null', triggering unintended LOG or REJECT actions on legitimate null-data requests?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: throttle(ContractCallRequest)] Can an unprivileged external user send a burst of requests with gas just above GAS_SCALE_FACTOR (10,001) so scaleGas() returns 1 token per request, consuming the gasLimitBucket at the minimum possible rate while still passing the gas check, effectively making the gas-based throttle equivalent to the request-rate throttle and defeating the purpose of having a separate gas-per-second limit?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: restore(long)] Can an unprivileged external user trigger the restore() path in ContractCallService.restoreGasToBucket() by submitting a transaction that reverts (not fails), causing Math.min(gasRemaining, gasLimitToRestoreBaseline) to restore more tokens than were actually consumed by the EVM, inflating the gasLimitBucket and allowing the attacker to submit more transactions than the throttle should permit?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: throttle(ContractCallRequest)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that @JsonIgnoreProperties(ignoreUnknown = true) on ContractCallRequest silently drops unknown JSON fields, allowing the attacker to include extra fields in the request body that are ignored by validation but could be used to probe the system's behavior or identify filter bypass opportunities without triggering any error response?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: throttle(ContractCallRequest)] Can an unprivileged external user set the from field to Address.ZERO (all zeros) in ContractCallRequest, bypassing the hasFrom() validation (which only requires from != null when value > 0), and then submit transactions that appear to originate from the zero address, potentially matching or avoiding FROM-based RequestFilter rules and impersonating a null sender in EVM execution?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: throttle(ContractCallRequest)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the RequestProperties.test() method where filters.isEmpty() returns true (no filters configured) causing the predicate to always return true for any request, meaning a REJECT action with no filters rejects ALL requests including legitimate ones, and an attacker who knows this configuration can craft requests to trigger a global denial of service by sending any request that matches the empty-filter rule?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: throttle(ContractCallRequest)] Can an unprivileged external user submit a ContractCallRequest with value > 0 and from set to a valid hex address but to set to empty string, exploiting the hasTo() validation which allows empty to when BytecodeUtils.isValidInitBytecode(data) is true, to submit contract-creation-like transactions that bypass to-address-based RequestFilter rules while still consuming gas from the shared throttle bucket?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: throttle(ContractCallRequest)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the non-atomic sequence of rateLimitBucket.tryConsume(1) followed by gasLimitBucket.tryConsume(scaleGas(gas)) in throttle() to perform a time-of-check-time-of-use (TOCTOU) attack where concurrent threads pass the rate limit check simultaneously before any of them consume from the gas bucket, allowing more requests than requestsPerSecond to be processed?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: throttle(ContractCallRequest)] Can an unprivileged external user craft a ContractCallRequest with a very large data field (maximum allowed hex string) that matches a RequestFilter with FilterField.DATA and FilterType.CONTAINS, causing the string comparison in FilterType.CONTAINS (using Strings.CI::contains) to perform expensive case-insensitive string operations on large inputs, leading to CPU exhaustion and degraded throttle enforcement for all users?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: restore(long)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the restore() logic in ContractController.call() where only InvalidParametersException triggers restore(request.getGas()), but other runtime exceptions (e.g., from contractExecutionService.processCall()) do not trigger restore(), causing the gasLimitBucket to be permanently depleted when unexpected exceptions occur, denying service to legitimate users?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: throttle(ContractCallRequest)] Can an unprivileged external user set gasPrice=0 in ContractCallRequest (allowed by @Min(0)) to submit zero-cost transactions that still consume gas tokens from gasLimitBucket proportional to the gas field, effectively using the throttle system as a free execution mechanism while other users paying non-zero gasPrice are equally throttled, creating an unfair resource allocation?
